result:
The first time I noticed the river talking was on a Tuesday so ordinary it felt laminated. I had taken the same gravel path to the same convenience store for the same plastic cup of burnt coffee, telling myself that repetition is a form of prayer. The sky was the color of a muted phone screen, and every leaf on the cottonwoods looked like it had been copied and pasted by a bored god. I wore the same fraying hoodie whose cuffs are histories of panic attacks—tiny holes I worry larger during Zoom meetings where no one knows I’m barefoot, scratching my ankle with the ballpoint pen I pretend is taking notes.  

Halfway to the store, the river cleared its throat. Not a human throat, more like a whole orchestra of throats made of water tuning itself before a concert no one bought tickets to. I stopped. The sound wasn’t on the soundtrack I associate with weekday errands; it sidled in between the hum of the highway overpass and the mechanical lullaby of the nearby car-wash, a frequency that made my fillings ache with nostalgia for something I hadn’t met yet.  

I have lived beside this slow brown coil of water for six years and never learned its name. Locals call it “the crick,” which sounds like a punchline you’re not allowed to laugh at. It’s too shallow for pride, too polluted for poetry, but that morning it pronounces my name better than my mother ever did, syllables braided with silt and shopping-cart bones.  

I knelt, because kneeling is what you do when the world suddenly speaks your language, and I plunged my burnt-coffee hand into the current. Cold slapped the bones in my wrist, an anesthesia wearing off in reverse. The water was thick with last night’s rain and this decade’s microplastics, yet a pulse traveled up my arm like Morse code scrawled by a lonely kid on a lighthouse wall. Translated, it said: You keep pretending you are separate.  

We all have a personal museum of embarrassments: the email sent at 3 a.m. confessing feelings to someone whose last name I misspelled; the time I tried to impress a date by claiming I understood quantum mechanics and then couldn’t calculate the tip; the poem I wrote in ninth grade that rhymed “abyss” with “kissing Chris.” But the loudest exhibit is the moment I realized I treat the planet like a rented room I don’t expect to get back the deposit on. I recycle when someone’s watching, buy organic when the sticker matches my aesthetic, mute the documentaries that show polar bears swimming until they dissolve into salt.  

The river kept talking, but softer now, like it knew I was the kind of coward who needs subtitles for confrontation. It said: Remember being seven at the beach, how you cried when your sandcastle was eaten by the tide, not because it was beautiful but because you thought the ocean was hungry for things you’d made? You were right. But the hunger is reciprocal.  

My sleeve was soaked to the elbow, and the convenience-store coffee had overturned, a caramel-colored Rorschach on the path suggesting lungs, or maybe wings. I should have felt ridiculous—an adult communing with urban runoff—but instead the laminated Tuesday cracked open like a geode, its cavity glinting with wet amethyst.  

I walked home pretending my veins glittered. Every house I passed exhaled its own secret: the retired couple who water their cement because green is a lifestyle; the teenager soldering a broken PlayStation, copper smoke braiding his hopes; the dog who barks the national anthem at sirens. I wanted to knock on each door and announce, “The river knows we’re sorry; we just don’t know how to say it back.” But apologies are currencies that deflate the moment you spend them publicly, so I kept walking.  

Inside my apartment I tried to transcribe the river’s syntax onto sticky notes. The best I could do was: “You are a borrowed constellation wearing biodegradable armor.” I stuck it on the fridge beside the magnet shaped like a pineapple that says “Stand Tall, Wear a Crown,” advice I’ve never managed to follow.  

Days accordion. Some collapse into others; some stretch like taffy you regret tasting. I started visiting the river at odd hours—5 a.m. when the heron stalked the parking-lot runoff like a detective in a noir novella; noon when the sun tried to sterilize the algae; midnight when bats scribbled calligraphy across the streetlights’ boredom. Each time, the water greeted me with new gossip: which turtle had grown a tumor shaped like the state of Florida, how a shopping cart had married a tire and they were expecting a flock of plastic bags. I never talked first; I’d sit on the concrete ledge and wait until the silence between us grew porous.  

Gradually, other listeners arrived. A graffiti artist who painted only endangered moths on the undersides of bridges. A widowed accountant feeding Cheetos to ducks while reciting tax codes like psalms. A runaway kid welding scrap into tiny guillotines for corporate logos. We nodded, strangers united by the suspicion that the world’s ending had already happened and we were the epilogue no one planned to print.  

One equinox, the river swelled high enough to kiss the warning signs that said “Stay Back—Fast Current.” We gathered instinctively, a circle of misfit punctuation marks. The accountant brought a beach umbrella printed with dollar signs; the kid arrived covered in metallic flecks; the artist carried a boom box playing whale sounds slowed to funeral tempo. I brought nothing but the hoodie now so threadbare it revealed constellation maps of my elbows.  

We waited. The river lifted its liquid chest and exhaled a single syllable that tasted like every birthday candle we’d ever blown out. Then it gave us what it could: a shopping cart surfaced, vines braided through its ribs like green CPR. On the wire cradle lay a turtle whose shell was cracked into a mosaic. One shard reflected my face, another the accountant’s dead wife, another the kid’s missing mother, another the artist’s felony record. In the cracked shell, we saw our broken parts fitted together into a shield no individual could carry alone.  

We didn’t discuss what to do. Language felt suddenly antique, a rotary phone trying to dial the moon. Instead, we waded in—jeans darkening, morality saturating—and hoisted the turtle onto the bank. The accountant whispered numbers that added up to forgiveness; the kid welded the shell with copper from pennies; the artist painted tiny galaxies over the scars; I held the animal’s gaze until it blinked once, slow as a decade. Then it lumbered back into the current, our collaborative artifact of damage and mercy.  

Afterward, we dispersed without exchanging contacts. The river kept talking, but now its voice included us—a subheading beneath the main paragraph. I still buy burnt coffee, still bite my cuffs during Zooms, still forget the river’s official name. But when I walk the gravel path, the laminated Tuesdays have hairline fractures through which something greener seeps. Some mornings I find tiny copper welds on the sidewalk shaped like turtle stitches; some nights I wake to the sound of shopping-cart bones rearranging themselves into a bridge I haven’t earned the right to cross.  

I no longer pretend separation. The river flows through my wrists when I type; the microplastics glitter in the emails I don’t send; the tide gnaws at every sandcastle I build out of unpaid bills and half-truths. Hunger is reciprocal, but so is tenderness. I cup it like water dripping from a busted faucet—impossible to hold, foolish to ignore, belonging to no one, offering to everyone.  

Outside, the ordinary Tuesday is already cracking open again. I shoulder the constellation of my biodegradable armor and step into the current, ready to be eaten, ready to be remade.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
